rss.title: NASA Science, Cargo Launch on 34th SpaceX Resupply Mission to Station
rss.snippet: The 34th SpaceX commercial resupply mission under contract with NASA is headed to the International Space Station with new scientific experiments after lifting off at 6:05 p.m. EDT Friday on a Falcon 9 rocket from Space Launch Complex 40 at Cape Canaveral Space Force Station in Florida. rss.title: Curiosity Shakes Loose a Pesky Rock
rss.snippet: After NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover drilled a sample from this rock on April 25, 2026, it withdrew its robotic arm and pulled the entire rock off the surface with it. Engineers spent several days repositioning the arm and vibrating the drill to try and get the rock loose. When it finally detached on M

rss.title: Hubble Sights Galaxy in Transition
rss.snippet: This NASA Hubble Space Telescope image reveals an enigmatic galaxy with a bright center and a face that hints at spiral structure, yet it holds no obvious spiral arms. Reddish-brown clumps and filaments of dust partially obscure the galaxy’s full face, while red, blue, and orange light from distant

github_repos.title: BarthPaleologue/CosmosJourneyer
github_repos.snippet: Cosmos Journeyer is a space exploration game running directly in the browser! Take your spaceship and witness the beauty of this virtually infinite universe.

youtube_items.title: [YT] Why is SpaceX Building the BIGGEST ROCKET EVER?
youtube_items.summary: I have a question. Why is SpaceX building the biggest rocket ever? Starship is 123 m tall. That's like a 40story building, taller than any other rocket in history. And it has two main sections. The spacecraft, which can carry an insane 150 tons of cargo, and an estimated 100 passengers. For comparison, the number of passengers for previous rockets is 4 to 8. And then there's the detachable rocket booster, which carries all the fuel and is powered by 33 of these engines. Each is twice as powerful as the ones used on the Falcon 9 rockets. Together, it's cost them over $5 billion and taken over 10 years to make. The plan is to prove that you can reuse the full Starship, which reduces launch costs, and then to use it to launch satellites and do cargo missions for NASA, and then help put humans

youtube_items.title: [YT] Why SpaceX and NASA Rockets Are So Different
youtube_items.summary: Let’s start by acknowledging something crucial.  Although NASA and SpaceX are both at the forefront   of space travel, their rockets look entirely  different, and that is mainly due to the fact that   they have very different goals. Imagine packing  for a trip to the edge of the solar system- that   is the kind of durability NASA is thinking  about. They need materials tough enough to   withstand the “unknown”. That is why NASA relies  on advanced materials like aluminum-lithium alloy,   known for being both lightweight and incredibly  strong. This allows their Space Launch System   (SLS) to survive intense forces of a rocket  launch, and the hostile environment of deep space.  Meanwhile, Sp

summary: Съвременните технологии за космически инфраструктури се развиват бързо, като SpaceX разработва гигантски ракети за повторна употреба, а Open Source проекти предлагат нови възможности за изследване и изкопаване на лунен грунт.
